title: Aortic Stenosis
oneLiner: A degenerative or congenital valve disease causing left ventricular outflow obstruction; a common cause of syncope, heart failure, and sudden death in the elderly.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Critical aortic stenosis with low output:** The murmur may become soft or absent as cardiac output falls. Classic clue: **very narrow pulse pressure (<30 mmHg)** + signs of cardiogenic shock (raised JVP, pulmonary oedema). Do not wait for echo — involve cardiology/cardiac surgical team urgently.
- **Aortic dissection with acute aortic regurgitation:** Sudden severe tearing chest/back pain, differential BP between arms, wide pulse pressure, new diastolic murmur. **Chest X-ray widened mediastinum ± ECG not showing STEMI** — this is not routine AS. Urgent CT aortogram and surgical referral.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Exertional dyspnoea (earliest)
  - Angina (despite clean coronaries — mismatch demand/supply)
  - Syncope or pre-syncope (especially on exertion — fixed stroke volume can't increase)
  - Fatigue, palpitations (atrial fibrillation common later)
  - Late: orthopnoea, PND (heart failure)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Carotid upstroke:** slow-rising, delayed peak (pulsus parvus et tardus) — most reliable physical sign
  - **Pulse pressure:** narrow (systolic low for age, diastolic relatively high)
  - **Apex beat:** sustained, non-displaced (LVH)
  - **Auscultation:** harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border (2nd ICS), radiating to carotids. S4 gallop. S2 may be soft or single (calcified valve)
  - **Late signs:** raised JVP, peripheral oedema, hepatomegaly (right heart failure)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM):** murmur increases with Valsalva (reduced preload), decreases with squatting. Carotid upstroke is brisk (not slow). ECG may show bizarre LVH with deep septal Q waves.
- **Mitral regurgitation:** blowing holosystolic murmur at apex, radiates to axilla, not carotids. Carotid upstroke normal or hyperdynamic.
- **Aortic sclerosis:** similar murmur but no symptoms, normal pulse character, normal pulse pressure. Differentiate by echo.
- **Pulmonary stenosis:** murmur at left upper sternal border, no radiation to carotids, often with RV lift.
- **Splitter:** **Carotid upstroke** — slow-rising (parvus et tardus) is AS; normal/brisk is HOCM or MR.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **ECG:** LVH with strain pattern (deep S V1-V2 + tall R V5-V6 with ST-T inversion). May show atrial fibrillation, left axis deviation.
  - **BP and pulse character** (listen to carotids as part of exam).
  - **Pulse oximetry** and **urine output** (if heart failure).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine (renal function for preload management and contrast risk)
  - Troponin (if angina — may be elevated in severe AS even without CAD)
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (elevated in heart failure, prognostic)
  - Blood cultures if fever (infective endocarditis suspect)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Echocardiogram (TTE):** first-line. Measures peak velocity, mean gradient, aortic valve area (AVA). **Severe:** peak velocity ≥4 m/s, mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, AVA ≤1.0 cm².
  - **Chest X-ray:** cardiomegaly (LVH), calcified aortic knob, pulmonary congestion if heart failure.
  - **CT coronary angiogram** pre-operative (to exclude coronary artery disease).
  - **Cardiac MRI** if discordant echo findings or bicuspid valve with aortopathy.
- **Special tests:**
  - Dobutamine stress echo if low-gradient severe AS with reduced LVEF (assess contractile reserve).
- **Diagnostic criteria (ESC/EACTS):**
  - Severe AS: any of: peak velocity ≥4 m/s, mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, AVA ≤1.0 cm², or AVA indexed ≤0.6 cm²/m².
- **Never-miss test:** **Echocardiogram** — in any patient with exertional syncope, angina, or dyspnoea plus a murmur. Omission risks missing critical AS and sudden death.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (hypotension, pulmonary oedema) — **intravenous fluid cautiously (250 mL bolus if preload dependent)** + call cardiology and cardiac surgery. **Avoid vasodilators (e.g., nitrates, ACE inhibitors)** — can cause catastrophic hypotension.
  - For pulmonary oedema: furosemide IV 20-40 mg [check local policy], but only if signs of volume overload. Do not diurese if JVP low.
  - Oxygen if SpO₂ <94%.
  - Urgent TTE.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor fluid balance, daily weights.
  - Diuretics for heart failure symptoms (furosemide PO 20-80 mg daily, titrate to euvolaemia).
  - Start beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol 1.25 mg OD) if angina or uncontrolled hypertension, but **avoid in acute decompensation**.
  - Arrange definitive intervention: **surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR)** or **transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI)** after multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion.
  - Treat atrial fibrillation (rate control with beta-blocker or digoxin, anticoagulate if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Valve replacement (SAVR if low-risk, TAVI if intermediate/high-risk or inoperable).
  - Medical therapy for inoperable: careful diuretics, avoid afterload reduction, consider low-dose beta-blocker if tolerated.
  - Infective endocarditis prophylaxis: only for prosthetic valves or prior IE (NICE 2023 no longer recommends for native valve disease alone).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at 1 mL/kg/h if euvolaemic (no fluid overload). Restrict if heart failure.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless high bleeding risk.
  - Diet: normal ward diet, low sodium (<2 g/day) if heart failure.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (1-hourly if unstable).
  - Activity: bed rest with chair if symptomatic; mobilise as tolerated if stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (symptom management):**
  - **Furosemide** (IV/PO): start 20-40 mg daily; titrate to euvolaemia. Max oral dose 120 mg BD (check local policy for high doses).
  - **Bisoprolol** (PO): 1.25-5 mg OD for rate control or angina; start low, titrate slowly.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Hydrochlorothiazide** (PO) 12.5-25 mg OD if mild fluid retention.
  - **Digoxin** 62.5-125 mcg PO OD (for AF rate control if beta-blocker contraindicated).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Vasodilators (ACEi, ARBs, nitrates, CCBs):** contraindicated in severe AS unless monitored in specialist setting — risk of hypotension and syncope.
  - **Beta-blockers:** avoid in acute heart failure and severe bradycardia; use with caution in COPD.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Furosemide dose increase in renal impairment (e.g., eGFR <30: may need 80-120 mg IV); monitor electrolytes.
  - Digoxin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (check levels).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Furosemide IV push too fast** (risk of ototoxicity) — give over 1-2 minutes.
  - **Nitrates for angina in severe AS** — can cause severe hypotension. Always check pulse character and BP before prescribing.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Syncope (low fixed stroke volume during exertion or vasodilation)
  - Pulmonary oedema (decompensated AS)
  - Cardiogenic shock (critical AS)
  - Infective endocarditis (rare, but higher risk with bicuspid valve)
- **Chronic:**
  - LV hypertrophy → diastolic dysfunction → heart failure
  - Atrial fibrillation (due to LA pressure overload)
  - Aortic regurgitation if valve calcification progresses asymmetrically
  - Sudden death (risk 1-2% per year in severe asymptomatic AS)
sections.Prognosis: - Symptom onset marks poor prognosis: angina → average survival 5 years; syncope → 3 years; heart failure → <2 years. Valve replacement restores near-normal survival. TAVI in inoperable patients reduces mortality by ~20% at 1 year.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Palpate the carotid upstroke in every patient with syncope** — it’s often more sensitive than the murmur for severe AS.
- **Check for bicuspid valve on echo** — if present, screen for ascending aortic aneurysm (CT/MRI).
- **Send blood cultures before starting antibiotics** in any AS patient with fever — endocarditis can present atypically.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not dismiss an aortic murmur as ‘sclerosis’ in an elderly patient with breathlessness** — always get an echo.
- **Do not give nitrates or ACE inhibitors for ‘angina/hypertension’ in undiagnosed AS** — may cause syncope.
- **Do not delay referral for valve replacement in symptomatic severe AS** — even frail patients benefit from TAVI.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness, new syncope or near-syncope, chest pain, palpitations with dizziness.
- **Follow-up:** outpatient cardiology within 2 weeks; echo within 3 months if not yet arranged.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant):**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - New onset syncope/chest pain on the ward
  - Pulmonary oedema not responding to diuretics
  - Suspected infective endocarditis (fever + new murmur)
  - Hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg) or signs of low output (oliguria, confusion, cold peripheries)
- **Call ICU / cardiac arrest team:**
  - Cardiac arrest (pulseless electrical activity or asystole common in severe AS)
  - Refractory cardiogenic shock
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or GCS <13

rawMarkdown:
## Aortic Stenosis
**One-liner:** A degenerative or congenital valve disease causing left ventricular outflow obstruction; a common cause of syncope, heart failure, and sudden death in the elderly.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Critical aortic stenosis with low output:** The murmur may become soft or absent as cardiac output falls. Classic clue: **very narrow pulse pressure (<30 mmHg)** + signs of cardiogenic shock (raised JVP, pulmonary oedema). Do not wait for echo — involve cardiology/cardiac surgical team urgently.
- **Aortic dissection with acute aortic regurgitation:** Sudden severe tearing chest/back pain, differential BP between arms, wide pulse pressure, new diastolic murmur. **Chest X-ray widened mediastinum ± ECG not showing STEMI** — this is not routine AS. Urgent CT aortogram and surgical referral.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Exertional dyspnoea (earliest)
  - Angina (despite clean coronaries — mismatch demand/supply)
  - Syncope or pre-syncope (especially on exertion — fixed stroke volume can't increase)
  - Fatigue, palpitations (atrial fibrillation common later)
  - Late: orthopnoea, PND (heart failure)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Carotid upstroke:** slow-rising, delayed peak (pulsus parvus et tardus) — most reliable physical sign
  - **Pulse pressure:** narrow (systolic low for age, diastolic relatively high)
  - **Apex beat:** sustained, non-displaced (LVH)
  - **Auscultation:** harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border (2nd ICS), radiating to carotids. S4 gallop. S2 may be soft or single (calcified valve)
  - **Late signs:** raised JVP, peripheral oedema, hepatomegaly (right heart failure)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM):** murmur increases with Valsalva (reduced preload), decreases with squatting. Carotid upstroke is brisk (not slow). ECG may show bizarre LVH with deep septal Q waves.
- **Mitral regurgitation:** blowing holosystolic murmur at apex, radiates to axilla, not carotids. Carotid upstroke normal or hyperdynamic.
- **Aortic sclerosis:** similar murmur but no symptoms, normal pulse character, normal pulse pressure. Differentiate by echo.
- **Pulmonary stenosis:** murmur at left upper sternal border, no radiation to carotids, often with RV lift.
- **Splitter:** **Carotid upstroke** — slow-rising (parvus et tardus) is AS; normal/brisk is HOCM or MR.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **ECG:** LVH with strain pattern (deep S V1-V2 + tall R V5-V6 with ST-T inversion). May show atrial fibrillation, left axis deviation.
  - **BP and pulse character** (listen to carotids as part of exam).
  - **Pulse oximetry** and **urine output** (if heart failure).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine (renal function for preload management and contrast risk)
  - Troponin (if angina — may be elevated in severe AS even without CAD)
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (elevated in heart failure, prognostic)
  - Blood cultures if fever (infective endocarditis suspect)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Echocardiogram (TTE):** first-line. Measures peak velocity, mean gradient, aortic valve area (AVA). **Severe:** peak velocity ≥4 m/s, mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, AVA ≤1.0 cm².
  - **Chest X-ray:** cardiomegaly (LVH), calcified aortic knob, pulmonary congestion if heart failure.
  - **CT coronary angiogram** pre-operative (to exclude coronary artery disease).
  - **Cardiac MRI** if discordant echo findings or bicuspid valve with aortopathy.
- **Special tests:**
  - Dobutamine stress echo if low-gradient severe AS with reduced LVEF (assess contractile reserve).
- **Diagnostic criteria (ESC/EACTS):**
  - Severe AS: any of: peak velocity ≥4 m/s, mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, AVA ≤1.0 cm², or AVA indexed ≤0.6 cm²/m².
- **Never-miss test:** **Echocardiogram** — in any patient with exertional syncope, angina, or dyspnoea plus a murmur. Omission risks missing critical AS and sudden death.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (hypotension, pulmonary oedema) — **intravenous fluid cautiously (250 mL bolus if preload dependent)** + call cardiology and cardiac surgery. **Avoid vasodilators (e.g., nitrates, ACE inhibitors)** — can cause catastrophic hypotension.
  - For pulmonary oedema: furosemide IV 20-40 mg [check local policy], but only if signs of volume overload. Do not diurese if JVP low.
  - Oxygen if SpO₂ <94%.
  - Urgent TTE.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor fluid balance, daily weights.
  - Diuretics for heart failure symptoms (furosemide PO 20-80 mg daily, titrate to euvolaemia).
  - Start beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol 1.25 mg OD) if angina or uncontrolled hypertension, but **avoid in acute decompensation**.
  - Arrange definitive intervention: **surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR)** or **transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI)** after multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion.
  - Treat atrial fibrillation (rate control with beta-blocker or digoxin, anticoagulate if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Valve replacement (SAVR if low-risk, TAVI if intermediate/high-risk or inoperable).
  - Medical therapy for inoperable: careful diuretics, avoid afterload reduction, consider low-dose beta-blocker if tolerated.
  - Infective endocarditis prophylaxis: only for prosthetic valves or prior IE (NICE 2023 no longer recommends for native valve disease alone).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at 1 mL/kg/h if euvolaemic (no fluid overload). Restrict if heart failure.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless high bleeding risk.
  - Diet: normal ward diet, low sodium (<2 g/day) if heart failure.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (1-hourly if unstable).
  - Activity: bed rest with chair if symptomatic; mobilise as tolerated if stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (symptom management):**
  - **Furosemide** (IV/PO): start 20-40 mg daily; titrate to euvolaemia. Max oral dose 120 mg BD (check local policy for high doses).
  - **Bisoprolol** (PO): 1.25-5 mg OD for rate control or angina; start low, titrate slowly.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Hydrochlorothiazide** (PO) 12.5-25 mg OD if mild fluid retention.
  - **Digoxin** 62.5-125 mcg PO OD (for AF rate control if beta-blocker contraindicated).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Vasodilators (ACEi, ARBs, nitrates, CCBs):** contraindicated in severe AS unless monitored in specialist setting — risk of hypotension and syncope.
  - **Beta-blockers:** avoid in acute heart failure and severe bradycardia; use with caution in COPD.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Furosemide dose increase in renal impairment (e.g., eGFR <30: may need 80-120 mg IV); monitor electrolytes.
  - Digoxin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (check levels).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Furosemide IV push too fast** (risk of ototoxicity) — give over 1-2 minutes.
  - **Nitrates for angina in severe AS** — can cause severe hypotension. Always check pulse character and BP before prescribing.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Syncope (low fixed stroke volume during exertion or vasodilation)
  - Pulmonary oedema (decompensated AS)
  - Cardiogenic shock (critical AS)
  - Infective endocarditis (rare, but higher risk with bicuspid valve)
- **Chronic:**
  - LV hypertrophy → diastolic dysfunction → heart failure
  - Atrial fibrillation (due to LA pressure overload)
  - Aortic regurgitation if valve calcification progresses asymmetrically
  - Sudden death (risk 1-2% per year in severe asymptomatic AS)

### Prognosis
- Symptom onset marks poor prognosis: angina → average survival 5 years; syncope → 3 years; heart failure → <2 years. Valve replacement restores near-normal survival. TAVI in inoperable patients reduces mortality by ~20% at 1 year.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Palpate the carotid upstroke in every patient with syncope** — it’s often more sensitive than the murmur for severe AS.
- **Check for bicuspid valve on echo** — if present, screen for ascending aortic aneurysm (CT/MRI).
- **Send blood cultures before starting antibiotics** in any AS patient with fever — endocarditis can present atypically.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not dismiss an aortic murmur as ‘sclerosis’ in an elderly patient with breathlessness** — always get an echo.
- **Do not give nitrates or ACE inhibitors for ‘angina/hypertension’ in undiagnosed AS** — may cause syncope.
- **Do not delay referral for valve replacement in symptomatic severe AS** — even frail patients benefit from TAVI.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness, new syncope or near-syncope, chest pain, palpitations with dizziness.
- **Follow-up:** outpatient cardiology within 2 weeks; echo within 3 months if not yet arranged.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant):**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - New onset syncope/chest pain on the ward
  - Pulmonary oedema not responding to diuretics
  - Suspected infective endocarditis (fever + new murmur)
  - Hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg) or signs of low output (oliguria, confusion, cold peripheries)
- **Call ICU / cardiac arrest team:**
  - Cardiac arrest (pulseless electrical activity or asystole common in severe AS)
  - Refractory cardiogenic shock
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or GCS <13
